findings.description: The CQI cycle diagram is present in both versions but appears smaller in the HTML rendering compared to the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a larger CQI cycle diagram centered on the page; HTML shows the same diagram but scaled down.

findings.description: Time range formatting uses en-dash in HTML vs em-dash style in PDF
findings.evidence: PDF uses '10:00 – 10:20:' with longer dash; HTML uses '10:00 – 10:20:' with slightly different dash character. Minor typographic difference.

findings.description: The horizontal rule/line under the title in the HTML version is not present in the PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML has a blue horizontal line separating the title from the content; PDF has no such line.

findings.description: Page 1 of PDF contains content through '11:45 – 12:15: Report out from Break Outs' while the HTML page 1 cuts off earlier, with remaining content flowing to page 2.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 ends at '11:45 – 12:15' section; HTML page 1 ends partway through the '10:00 – 10:20' section with the rest continuing on HTML page 2.

findings.description: The time block headers in the HTML use slightly less prominent bold formatting but are still distinguishable as headings.
findings.evidence: PDF shows '10:40 – 11:15: Identify the Problem:' with larger bold text; HTML shows similar bold text but at a smaller relative size.

findings.description: The gap between '12:15' (Report out from Break Outs) and '12:30' (Working Lunch) sections spans a page break in the PDF but flows continuously in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has page break between pages 1 and 2 separating these sections; HTML flows them continuously on page 2.

findings.description: The HTML contains a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, which is a structural duplication of the wrapper element, though no content is duplicated.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> wrapping another <main> - this is a structural issue but does not cause content duplication.
